title.en: MECE tree: diagnosing a drop in card approval rates for 25–34 year-olds
title.es: Árbol MECE: diagnóstico de una caída en las tasas de aprobación de tarjetas para personas de 25 a 34 años

prompt.en: Build an issue tree for: 'Why are Capital One card approval rates dropping in the 25–34 age band?' Show two levels of decomposition and flag which branch you would pursue first and why.
prompt.es: Construye un árbol de asuntos para: '¿Por qué están cayendo las tasas de aprobación de tarjetas de Capital One en el segmento de 25 a 34 años?' Muestra dos niveles de descomposición e indica qué rama perseguirías primero y por qué.

must_include: first-level split is MECE — a clean binary or exhaustive partition of the approval rate metric, e.g., (1) applicant pool quality changed vs. (2) Capital One's underwriting standards changed; or (1) demand-side vs. (2) supply-side; both halves together fully explain all possible causes

second level under each first-level branch adds at least 2 concrete sub-drivers specific to the 25–34 context — e.g., under 'applicant pool quality': credit score distribution shift, debt-to-income ratio change driven by student loan burden; under 'underwriting standards': model recalibration, regulatory change tightening cutoffs

identifies which first-level branch to pursue first and explains the reasoning — e.g., 'I'd start with underwriting standards because a policy or model change would affect all 25–34 applicants uniformly, which is a more testable and faster-to-validate hypothesis than a population-level credit shift'

acknowledges at least one scenario where this tree structure would need to be rebuilt — e.g., 'if approval rates are dropping only in certain geographies or through certain channels, I'd need to add a channel/geo dimension at level one'

first-level split passes the MECE test explicitly — candidate should confirm that the two branches are mutually exclusive (a cause cannot sit in both) and collectively exhaustive (all possible causes land in one of the two)
must_avoid: first-level split that conflates supply-side and demand-side causes — e.g., listing 'economic conditions, credit model, applicant behavior, regulatory pressure' as four parallel level-one nodes without proving they are MECE
second-level nodes that repeat the first-level label — e.g., under 'underwriting' listing 'stricter underwriting' as a sub-driver (circular, not decomposed)
skipping the prioritization step and treating all branches as equally urgent
a tree that is complete at level one but stops — two levels are explicitly required
attributing the drop solely to macro conditions without exploring Capital One's internal levers as a parallel hypothesis
